unitTitle: KS2 Y5 Selection in quizzes (Teach Computing — adapted)

lessons.title: Exploring conditions
lessons.objectives:
I can say what a condition is
I can find the condition in a Scratch program
I can change a condition and see what happens
lessons.outline:
Routine first (same every lesson). Vocabulary on board: selection · condition · true · false. TA: everyone has the starter + activity worksheet; Scratch open with the 'Modifying selection' project. Read the 3 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — true or false. Pupils sort statements into true/false on the starter worksheet to meet the idea that a condition is checked and is either true or false.
2. (5–20) I-do → we-do — what is a condition. Show the 'key r pressed' program: the condition is the part the computer CHECKS; the action runs when it is true. Likely error: pupils point at the action → fix-words “the condition is the part the computer checks.”
   • Support: choose which block is the condition.  • Core: write an action that runs when true.  • Challenge: write their own true condition.
3. (20–35) You-do — change a condition. Pupils open the Scratch project, change a condition (e.g. the key), run it, and screenshot onto the activity worksheet. TA: prompt, do not do it for them.
4. (35–45) Plenary — self-tick the 3 “I can…”; each pupil points at a condition on screen.
Differentiation on the SAME task, low floor / high ceiling — nobody’s screen looks “less done”. No flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Selecting outcomes
lessons.objectives:
I can use a forever loop so the condition is checked again and again
I can find the condition and the two outcomes in if… then… else…
I can make a program that uses selection to produce different outcomes
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: condition · true · false · outcome · if… then… else…. TA: starter + activity worksheet; Scratch open with the 'Using repeats' project. Read the 3 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — why a forever loop. Pupils compare the with-loop and no-loop images on the starter worksheet and choose which keeps checking. Likely error: thinking a key press always works → fix-words “no loop = checked once.”
2. (5–20) I-do → we-do — two outcomes. Introduce if… then… else…: THEN = the true outcome, ELSE = the false outcome. Act it out (touch nose → thumbs up, else thumbs down). Read the two outcomes in the key-a program.
   • Support: choose when the else runs.  • Core: write what happens if the key is not pressed.  • Challenge: name their own two outcomes.
3. (20–38) You-do — programming two outcomes. Pupils order the if/then/else structure, then build a program with a condition and TWO outcomes in Scratch (keeping the forever loop), and screenshot it. TA: prompt, do not do it for them.
4. (38–45) Plenary — self-tick the 3 “I can…”; each pupil names their then and else outcomes.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Asking questions
lessons.objectives:
I can ask a question in Scratch with ask and wait
I can use the answer as the condition
I can make the program go two ways (true and false)
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: question · answer · condition · true · false · outcome. TA: starter + activity worksheet; Scratch open with the 'Program flow' project. Read the 3 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — binary questions. Pupils sort questions into yes/no and not-yes/no on the starter worksheet; meet that the answer becomes the condition.
2. (5–20) I-do → we-do — ask and wait. Model the ask () and wait block (no forever loop needed — ask waits). Read the 'Would you like me to grow?' program: yes → true outcome, anything else → false outcome.
   • Support: who types the answer.  • Core: why “ten” is false when the condition is “answer = 10”.  • Challenge: write their own yes/no question.
3. (20–25) Exact match — sort answers into TRUE/FALSE for “answer = 10”. Likely error: expecting “ten” to count → fix-words “the computer checks the exact letters and numbers.”
4. (25–40) You-do — build a program that asks a question and goes two ways, then screenshot it. Support: the scaffolded 'Programming different outcomes (support)' project. TA: prompt, do not do it for them.
5. (40–45) Plenary — self-tick the 3 “I can…”; each pupil shows their question and two outcomes.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Designing a quiz
lessons.objectives:
I can read the quiz task and say what it needs
I can plan my quiz questions and answers on a design sheet
I can work out which outcome each answer gives
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: task · design · question · answer · condition · outcome. TA: starter + activity worksheet; the design sheets ready. No code today — this is a planning lesson. Read the 3 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — the parts of a quiz program. Pupils read the 'Is 56 in the eight times table?' quiz on the starter worksheet and name the question and the correct answer (the condition).
2. (5–20) I-do → we-do — the task and the parts. Read the task: make an interactive quiz that uses selection. Label the quiz program parts (question · condition · TRUE outcome · FALSE outcome).
   • Support: choose the outcome for a yes answer.  • Core: write the outcome for a wrong answer.  • Challenge: write a good question + answer.
3. (20–38) You-do — plan the quiz. Pupils complete at least two questions on the design sheet (question, correct answer, correct outcome, wrong outcome) and screenshot/photograph their plan. Support: give a topic. TA: prompt, do not do it for them.
4. (38–45) Plenary — follow given answers (6, 11, 24, 4) through a plan and choose the outcome each time; self-tick the 3 “I can…”.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

resources.title: Quiz: is 56 in the eight times table? (Teach Computing).png
resources.sourceAttribution: Teach Computing Curriculum © Raspberry Pi Foundation, OGL v3.0.

lessons.title: Testing a quiz
lessons.objectives:
I can build the first question of my quiz in Scratch
I can test my quiz with a right answer and a wrong answer
I can share my quiz and give kind feedback
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: implement · program · test · run · debug. TA: pupils’ designs from last lesson; the starter + activity worksheet; Scratch open; sticky notes for feedback. Read the 3 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — both, one, or none. Pupils compare Program A (Paris, if… then… else…) and Program B (8×7, if-then only) on the starter worksheet.
2. (5–25) I-do → we-do — build the first question. Model: welcome → ask the first question → check the answer with if… then… else… → add TRUE and FALSE outcomes. Watch the demo video first if useful. Stop and test before adding more.
   • Support: a debugging tick-list (multi-select).  • Core: name one thing to check when a wrong answer misbehaves.  • Challenge: add and test a second question.
3. (25–40) You-do — test and debug. Run with a right answer, then a wrong answer; the output must match the plan. Likely error: outcome in the wrong part of the block → fix-words “true goes in then, false goes in else.” Screenshot the working quiz. TA: prompt, do not do it for them.
4. (40–45) Share — swap devices, try a partner’s quiz, leave one kind comment. Self-tick the 3 “I can…”.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Evaluating a quiz
lessons.objectives:
I can find a way my quiz could be improved
I can add setup so the quiz starts the same every time
I can improve my quiz in Scratch
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: evaluate · improve · setup · condition · outcome. TA: pupils’ quizzes from last lesson; the starter + activity worksheet; Scratch open. Read the 3 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — spot the difference. Pupils compare two near-identical programs (answer = no vs answer = yes) and name the single difference (the condition).
2. (5–18) I-do → we-do — the matching problem. Users who knew the answers got them wrong because they typed them differently. The answer must match EXACTLY. Likely error: expecting “Correct!” to equal “correct” → fix-words “the computer checks the exact letters.”
   • Support: when does setup run.  • Core: why “set colour effect to 0” must be in setup.  • Challenge: one more improvement.
3. (18–35) You-do — add setup + improve. Pupils tick the setup blocks they need (multi-select), add a setup to their quiz so every user gets the same start, tell users how to answer, test as they go, and screenshot. TA: prompt, do not do it for them.
4. (35–45) Evaluate (plenary) — how did you meet the task, what worked, what you improved, what could be better. A scoring system uses variables (Year 6). Recap the unit journey: condition → if… then… else… → ask → quiz. Self-tick the 3 “I can…”.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.
